events.text: The patient is a 47-year-old female named Marguerite Okonkwo. She is of West African descent (Nigerian-American). She is a non-smoker with no significant alcohol use. She works as a high school principal. She has a significant family history notable for her mother dying of ovarian cancer at age 55 and a maternal aunt with breast cancer diagnosed at age 42. She is G2P2 with two children, both delivered vaginally. She has no prior personal history of malignancy. She has a history of mild hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, and well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine.

At age 47 years, the patient was diagnosed with HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA of the OVARY (bilateral), FIGO Stage IIIC, AJCC Stage IIIC (T3cN1M0). Primary site: Ovary, bilateral (ICD-O site code C56.9). Histology: Serous cystadenocarcinoma, high-grade (ICD-O histology code 8461/3). The tumor demonstrated diffuse peritoneal carcinomatosis with miliary implants throughout the omentum, right and left paracolic gutters, pelvic peritoneum, and sigmoid mesentery. CT imaging at diagnosis identified a large right adnexal mass measuring 9.2 x 7.4 cm, a left adnexal mass measuring 4.8 x 3.9 cm, and massive ascites (approximately 4.5 liters by clinical estimate). No distant (M1) disease identified. Regional lymph nodes: bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy (right external iliac node 2.1 cm, right para-aortic node cluster 1.8 cm). Serum CA-125 at diagnosis: 4,872 U/mL (markedly elevated). CEA: 2.1 ng/mL (within normal limits). HE4: 412 pmol/L (markedly elevated). ROMA score: High risk (postmenopausal equivalent used). Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY (omental biopsy and peritoneal implant biopsy at time of debulking). Tumor Size: Right ovarian primary 9.2 cm. Summary Stage: Regional (peritoneal spread). Performance Status: ECOG 1.

At age 47 years, the patient presented to her gynecologist with a 3-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and new-onset pelvic pressure. She also reported a 9-pound unintentional weight loss over 2 months and intermittent right-sided pelvic pain radiating to the right lower back. Physical examination was notable for a distended abdomen with shifting dullness consistent with ascites, and a right adnexal mass palpable on bimanual examination. Pelvic ultrasound performed at the gynecologist's office revealed complex bilateral adnexal masses with internal septations, papillary projections, and large-volume free fluid. The patient was urgently referred to gynecologic oncology. ECOG performance status 1. The patient expressed significant anxiety regarding her family history and was counseled about the role of genetic testing.

At age 47 years, the patient underwent CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast (staging CT). Findings: Large complex right adnexal mass measuring 9.2 x 7.4 x 8.1 cm with mixed solid and cystic components, internal papillary projections, and irregular thick septations. Left adnexal mass measuring 4.8 x 3.9 x 4.1 cm with similar morphology. Large-volume ascites throughout the abdomen and pelvis. Omental caking noted - diffuse infiltration and thickening of the greater omentum, most prominent in the right upper quadrant (omental thickness up to 3.2 cm). Peritoneal implants: Multiple soft tissue nodules along the right and left paracolic gutters, pelvic peritoneum, sigmoid mesentery, and right hemidiaphragm (largest 1.4 cm). Right external iliac lymph node 2.1 cm (enlarged). Para-aortic lymph node cluster at L2 level measuring 1.8 cm aggregate. No evidence of liver parenchymal metastases. No pleural effusion. No pulmonary nodules on lower lung bases. CT chest (dedicated): No pulmonary metastases, no mediastinal adenopathy, no pleural effusion. Impression: Bilateral ovarian masses with peritoneal carcinomatosis, omental caking, pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy, and massive ascites. CT findings are highly suspicious for advanced-stage ovarian carcinoma. Cancer status: CANCER PRESENT. Primary site and metastatic peritoneal disease identified.

At age 47 years, the patient underwent diagnostic paracentesis (therapeutic/diagnostic) yielding 2.8 liters of straw-colored ascitic fluid. Cytology of ascitic fluid: POSITIVE FOR MALIGNANT CELLS. Morphology: Papillary clusters and single cells with enlarged, pleomorphic nuclei, prominent nucleoli, and high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratios. Cell block prepared. Immunohistochemistry on cell block: PAX8 positive (diffuse nuclear), WT1 positive (diffuse nuclear), CK7 positive, CK20 negative, ER focally positive, p53 diffuse strong nuclear positivity (aberrant pattern consistent with TP53 mutation), p16 diffuse block positivity. Ki-67 proliferative index: approximately 70%. Morphology and immunophenotype consistent with HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, most likely of ovarian/fallopian tube primary. BRCA1 immunohistochemistry: Loss of nuclear BRCA1 expression in tumor cells (positive internal control in stromal cells). Pathologic diagnosis: Malignant serous carcinoma in peritoneal fluid, high-grade.

At age 47 years, the patient had germline genetic testing performed (blood-based germline panel via Myriad myRisk Hereditary Cancer panel, 35-gene panel). Results: PATHOGENIC VARIANT DETECTED - BRCA1 c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74), also known as BRCA1 5382insC, a founder mutation prevalent in individuals of Ashkenazi Jewish and some Eastern European descent, but also identified in other populations. This is classified as a Class 5 Pathogenic Variant. Variant allele frequency: 48% (consistent with germline heterozygosity). The patient was also found to carry a BRCA2 variant of uncertain significance (VUS): BRCA2 c.8167G>A (p.Asp2723Asn) - clinical significance uncertain, not actionable at this time. Additionally, somatic tumor tissue next-generation sequencing was performed on the omental biopsy specimen (Foundation One CDx panel, 324 genes). Somatic findings: TP53 p.R175H mutation (pathogenic, 68% VAF - consistent with loss of heterozygosity), BRCA1 somatic loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at BRCA1 locus (consistent with second-hit inactivation of germline BRCA1), RB1 loss (homozygous deletion), CCNE1 amplification (copy number 12), PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (VAF 14%, likely subclonal), NF1 frameshift deletion p.Q1846Lfs*17 (VAF 22%), NOTCH3 missense p.P1155S (VUS, VAF 18%), FAT1 truncating mutation (VUS), CDK12 amplification (copy number 6). No KRAS, NRAS, BRAF mutations detected. No MSI-high or TMB-high. Homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score: 62 (above threshold of 42, HRD positive). Tumor mutational burden: 6.2 mutations/Mb. Microsatellite status: MSS. PD-L1 expression: 5% (CPS). Actionable alterations: BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant with somatic LOH (PARP inhibitor sensitive), HRD positive.

At age 47 years, the patient was seen in gynecologic oncology consultation. Multidisciplinary tumor board reviewed the case. Given FIGO stage IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with good performance status (ECOG 1), the tumor board recommended PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS) with goal of complete gross resection (R0). The patient was deemed a good surgical candidate by anesthesia pre-operative evaluation. Pre-operative CA-125: 4,872 U/mL. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant confirmed. Genetic counseling completed. Patient counseled regarding hereditary implications for her two children (50% risk for each child), and referral made for children to be seen by genetic counseling when appropriate. Prophylactic risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) for at-risk family members discussed. Surgical consent obtained. Patient psychologically supported with referral to oncology social work.

At age 47 years, the patient underwent PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS) via midline laparotomy. Procedure: Bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, radical omentectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, appendectomy, resection of sigmoid mesenteric implants, stripping of right hemidiaphragm peritoneum, and multiple peritoneal implant resections. Intraoperative findings: Bilateral adnexal masses as described on imaging. Diffuse miliary peritoneal carcinomatosis. Omental cake - complete omentum replaced by tumor. Approximately 4.0 liters of ascites evacuated. Sigmoid mesenteric implants (2 implants, largest 1.6 cm) resected with negative margins. Right hemidiaphragm peritoneum stripped for a 6x5 cm area with tumor involvement. Lymph node dissection yielded 38 pelvic nodes (4 positive for carcinoma, largest 2.1 cm right external iliac) and 22 para-aortic nodes (2 positive, both at infrarenal level). No bowel resection required. FINAL RESECTION STATUS: R0 - Complete gross resection achieved, no macroscopic residual disease. Estimated blood loss: 850 mL. Intraoperative transfusion: 2 units pRBCs. Total operative time: 6 hours 15 minutes. Post-operative course: Uncomplicated. ICU overnight for monitoring. Returned to floor on POD 1. Ileus resolved by POD 3. DVT prophylaxis with enoxaparin initiated. Discharged POD 6 in stable condition.

At age 47 years, the patient's primary debulking surgery specimen was reviewed by gynecologic pathology. FINAL SURGICAL PATHOLOGY: Right ovary and fallopian tube: HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, right ovarian primary, 9.2 cm greatest dimension, involving the ovarian surface, fallopian tube serosa, and fallopian tube fimbriae. Fallopian tube: Serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) identified at the fimbrial end of the right fallopian tube (consistent with fallopian tube as site of origin). Left ovary and fallopian tube: Involvement by high-grade serous carcinoma, 4.5 cm, with STIC in left fallopian tube fimbriae. Uterus: Serosal surface implants of high-grade serous carcinoma; endometrium and myometrium uninvolved. Omentum: Extensive involvement by high-grade serous carcinoma (omental cake). Peritoneal implants (multiple sites): High-grade serous carcinoma at all sites submitted. Appendix: Serosal surface implant of carcinoma, appendiceal lumen uninvolved. Right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping: High-grade serous carcinoma present, deep margin negative (0.5 mm). Lymph nodes: Right pelvic (4/18 positive), left pelvic (0/20 positive), para-aortic infrarenal (2/12 positive), para-aortic suprarenal (0/10 positive). Surgical margins: All resection margins negative. Residual disease: None identified (R0). IHC confirmation: WT1+, PAX8+, p53 aberrant, ER focally+, CK7+, CK20-. FINAL PATHOLOGIC STAGE: pT3cN1M0, FIGO Stage IIIC.

At age 47 years, 4 weeks post-operatively, the patient was seen in gynecologic oncology follow-up. She had recovered well from surgery. Surgical wound healing was complete. Abdominal examination: soft, no distension, no ascites clinically. CA-125 post-operative (pre-chemotherapy): 843 U/mL (significant decline from 4,872 U/mL pre-operatively, but still markedly elevated, consistent with residual microscopic disease). ECOG performance status: 1. Labs: WBC 7.2, Hgb 10.8 (mild anemia, recovering from surgical blood loss), Plt 312, Cr 0.8, LFTs normal. The patient was counseled regarding the plan for adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy. Given her germline BRCA1 status and R0 resection, the treatment plan discussed included: 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel followed by PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy (niraparib per PRIMA/ENGOT-OV16 data) and consideration of bevacizumab. After discussion of toxicity profiles and patient preference (she preferred to avoid bevacizumab-associated toxicities including hypertension given her pre-existing hypertension), the plan was finalized as carboplatin AUC 6/paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 q21 days x 6 cycles, followed by niraparib maintenance. Chemotherapy to begin in 2 weeks (6 weeks post-op).

At age 47 years, 6 weeks following primary debulking surgery, the patient initiated ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY with curative intent: Carboplatin AUC 6 / Paclitaxel 175 mg/m2, administered intravenously every 21 days. BSA: 1.74 m2. Carboplatin dose: 612 mg (calculated using Calvert formula with GFR 68 mL/min by CKD-EPI). Paclitaxel dose: 304 mg. Premedications: Dexamethasone 20 mg IV, diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, famotidine 20 mg IV (hypersensitivity prophylaxis). Paclitaxel administered over 3 hours. Carboplatin administered over 30 minutes. Port-a-cath placed prior to cycle 1 for venous access. Cycle 1 was completed without acute infusion reactions. The patient tolerated the initial infusion well. Treatment goal: Adjuvant therapy following R0 primary debulking surgery, targeting CA-125 normalization and microscopic disease eradication.

At age 47 years, during cycle 2 of carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy (approximately 3 weeks after cycle 1), the patient reported GRADE 2 PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY - bilateral distal upper and lower extremity numbness and tingling, described as a 'stocking and glove' distribution. Also reported: Grade 1 alopecia (diffuse hair thinning, expected). Grade 1 nausea managed with ondansetron and prochlorperazine prn. Grade 1 fatigue. No hypersensitivity reaction. CBC: WBC 3.8 (Grade 1 neutropenia), ANC 1.9, Hgb 10.1, Plt 198. Neuropathy was managed with counseling, dose held assessment (not required at Grade 2), and acetyl-L-carnitine supplement initiated per patient preference. Neurology referral deferred at this time. Paclitaxel continued at full dose given Grade 2 (not Grade 3) neuropathy. CA-125 cycle 2 day 1: 287 U/mL (significant decline from 843 U/mL, indicating response to therapy).

At age 47 years, after cycle 3 of carboplatin/paclitaxel (interim restaging CT), the patient underwent CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast. Findings: Complete resolution of ascites. No evidence of residual adnexal masses (bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy performed). Post-surgical changes in the pelvis and abdomen consistent with prior debulking surgery. No evidence of peritoneal implants or omental nodularity. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. No new hepatic lesions. No pleural effusion. CT chest: No pulmonary metastases. Impression: No radiographic evidence of residual or recurrent disease following primary debulking surgery and 3 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel. COMPLETE RADIOGRAPHIC RESPONSE at interim restaging. Cancer status: NO CANCER IDENTIFIED on imaging. Responding to therapy.

At age 47 years, during cycle 4 of carboplatin/paclitaxel, the patient experienced a GRADE 2 HYPERSENSITIVITY REACTION TO CARBOPLATIN during infusion - onset approximately 12 minutes into the carboplatin infusion: flushing, urticaria, chest tightness, and pruritus. Blood pressure remained stable (no anaphylaxis). The infusion was immediately stopped. The patient was treated with diphenhydramine 50 mg IV, hydrocortisone 100 mg IV, and epinephrine 0.3 mg IM was prepared but not required. Symptoms resolved within 20 minutes. After a 60-minute observation period, carboplatin was re-challenged at a slower infusion rate (2 hours instead of 30 minutes) with additional premedication (cetirizine added, dexamethasone dose increased to 40 mg). The re-challenge was completed successfully without recurrence of reaction. Allergist/immunology consulted; recommended extended premedication protocol for all future carboplatin infusions (12-step carboplatin desensitization protocol considered for cycle 5-6). Cycle 4 carboplatin delivered at full dose via extended desensitization.

At age 47 years, after completing cycle 4 of carboplatin/paclitaxel, the patient was evaluated in clinic. Performance status ECOG 1. Peripheral neuropathy has stabilized at Grade 1-2 (improved from prior Grade 2). The carboplatin hypersensitivity was managed successfully. The patient completed cycle 5 and cycle 6 of carboplatin/paclitaxel using the 12-step carboplatin desensitization protocol without further hypersensitivity events. CA-125 after cycle 4: 48 U/mL. CA-125 after cycle 6 (end of treatment): 11 U/mL (NORMALIZED, within normal limits of <35 U/mL). HE4: 58 pmol/L (normalized). The patient tolerated all 6 cycles, with cumulative Grade 2 neuropathy (stable, not requiring dose reduction of paclitaxel) and managed carboplatin hypersensitivity. Alopecia Grade 2 (complete hair loss). End-of-treatment CBC: WBC 5.1, ANC 3.2, Hgb 11.6, Plt 287. ECOG performance status improved to 0. Discussion of maintenance therapy initiated.

At age 47 years, end-of-chemotherapy restaging imaging (post cycle 6 carboplatin/paclitaxel): CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast and CT chest. Findings: No evidence of disease. Post-surgical and post-treatment changes. No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. No hepatic lesions. No pleural effusion. CT chest: No pulmonary nodules, no mediastinal adenopathy, no pleural effusion. Impression: COMPLETE RADIOGRAPHIC RESPONSE maintained. No evidence of residual or recurrent ovarian carcinoma. Cancer status: NO CANCER IDENTIFIED. Complete response to therapy.

At age 48 years (approximately 8 months after initial diagnosis), following completion of 6 cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel with CA-125 normalization and complete radiographic response, the patient initiated NIRAPARIB MAINTENANCE THERAPY (PARP inhibitor, oral). Indication: First-line maintenance therapy in BRCA1-mutated high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma in complete response following platinum-based chemotherapy (per PRIMA/ENGOT-OV16 trial data and NCCN guidelines). Baseline body weight: 63 kg. Baseline platelet count: 287,000/mcL. Per PRIMA individualized starting dose criteria: Weight <77 kg AND platelet count <150,000 not applicable (platelets above threshold). However, given weight <77 kg (63 kg), patient dosed at NIRAPARIB 200 mg orally once daily (individualized starting dose per FDA label, rather than 300 mg) to reduce toxicity risk. Baseline CBC: WBC 5.1, Hgb 11.6, Plt 287. Baseline creatinine: 0.79. Baseline LFTs: normal. Patient counseled on common toxicities including nausea, fatigue, thrombocytopenia, anemia, and rare risk of myelodysplastic syndrome/AML. Nausea prophylaxis: ondansetron 8 mg PO 30 minutes prior to each niraparib dose (bedtime dosing recommended).

At age 48 years, approximately 4 weeks into niraparib maintenance therapy, the patient presented for routine laboratory monitoring. CBC showed: WBC 4.2, Hgb 11.0, Plt 98,000/mcL. GRADE 2 THROMBOCYTOPENIA (platelet count 50,000-99,999/mcL per CTCAE v5.0). The patient reported no bleeding symptoms (no petechiae, ecchymoses, epistaxis, or gingival bleeding). Physical examination: no petechiae, no organomegaly. The niraparib dose was held per protocol for 28 days (to allow platelet recovery). Repeat CBC at 1 week: Plt 134,000/mcL (recovering). Repeat CBC at 2 weeks: Plt 187,000/mcL (recovered above 100,000/mcL threshold). Niraparib was restarted at a REDUCED DOSE of NIRAPARIB 100 mg orally once daily (one dose level reduction from 200 mg). Hematology consulted to rule out alternative etiology of thrombocytopenia; bone marrow biopsy deferred given clinical context (PARP inhibitor-associated thrombocytopenia most likely). Peripheral blood smear: No schistocytes, no blasts. Reticulocyte count: normal. B12, folate, iron studies: normal.

At age 48 years, 6 weeks after restarting niraparib at 100 mg daily (reduced dose), the patient was seen in follow-up. Tolerating the reduced dose well. No bleeding symptoms. CBC: WBC 4.8, Hgb 11.4, Plt 156,000/mcL (Grade 0 thrombocytopenia - platelet count stable above 100,000/mcL). Continuing niraparib at 100 mg daily - no further dose reduction required. CA-125: 9 U/mL (within normal limits). HE4: 54 pmol/L (normal). ECOG performance status: 0. Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 1, improved, stable. Alopecia has resolved with hair regrowth noted. The patient reports mild fatigue (Grade 1), nausea manageable with ondansetron (Grade 1). Quality of life assessment: good, returned to work full-time as school principal. Psychosocial support ongoing. No symptoms of recurrence. Plan: Continue niraparib 100 mg daily, CBC monitoring every 4 weeks, CA-125 and clinical assessment every 3 months.

At age 48 years, approximately 3 months into niraparib 100 mg daily (after dose reduction), the patient was noted to have GRADE 1 ANEMIA on routine CBC: Hgb 9.8 g/dL (Grade 1: 10.0 to LLN). No symptoms attributable to anemia. Niraparib continued at 100 mg daily without dose modification (Grade 1 anemia does not require dose reduction per label). Iron studies: Ferritin 12 ng/mL, serum iron 44 mcg/dL, TIBC 390 mcg/dL - consistent with concurrent IRON DEFICIENCY ANEMIA. Oral iron supplementation initiated (ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO BID). Repeat CBC at 6 weeks: Hgb 10.6 g/dL (improving). The iron deficiency was attributed to surgical blood loss and chemotherapy-related erythropoietic suppression compounded by niraparib. No erythropoietin-stimulating agent required. The patient tolerated oral iron with mild GI upset, which was managed with dosing with food.

At age 48 years, approximately 6 months into niraparib maintenance therapy (14 months from initial diagnosis), the patient underwent surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast and CT chest as part of routine surveillance. Findings: No evidence of disease. Post-surgical changes as before. No ascites. No peritoneal implants or omental nodularity. No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes. No hepatic lesions. No adnexal masses. CT chest: No pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion, no mediastinal adenopathy. Impression: NO EVIDENCE OF DISEASE. Complete response maintained on niraparib maintenance therapy. Cancer status: NO CANCER IDENTIFIED. No evidence of recurrence. CA-125 at time of imaging: 8 U/mL (well within normal limits).

At age 48 years, the patient continues on niraparib 100 mg daily maintenance therapy with excellent tolerance at the reduced dose. She is 14 months from initial diagnosis and 8 months into maintenance therapy. CA-125 remains normalized at 8 U/mL. ECOG performance status: 0. She has returned to full-time work and reports good quality of life. CBC stable: WBC 5.0, Hgb 11.2, Plt 162,000/mcL. Peripheral neuropathy has improved to Grade 0-1 (barely perceptible). She underwent genetic counseling with her two adult children (ages 22 and 19); both children opted for germline BRCA1 testing - results pending at this visit. Bone density (DXA scan): T-score -1.2 (low normal/osteopenia) - initiated calcium 1200 mg + vitamin D 1000 IU daily supplementation. Gynecologic oncology follow-up scheduled every 3 months. Patient was informed about the SOLO2 and STUDY 19 trial data supporting long-term maintenance. Referral maintained with genetic counseling for ongoing family management. Plan: Continue niraparib maintenance for 3 years total (per PRIMA protocol duration) barring progression or intolerable toxicity.

At age 48 years (approximately 18 months from diagnosis, 12 months of niraparib maintenance), the patient presents for routine surveillance visit. She reports excellent quality of life. No symptoms suggestive of recurrence (no abdominal distension, no pelvic pain, no early satiety, no new GI symptoms). CA-125: 10 U/mL (stable, within normal limits). Physical examination: No palpable masses, no ascites, no peripheral lymphadenopathy, post-surgical abdomen without concerning findings. CBC: WBC 4.9, Hgb 11.5, Plt 172,000/mcL (stable, niraparib well-tolerated at 100 mg dose). Fatigue Grade 0-1 (minimal). Nausea fully resolved. The patient's oldest child (age 22) tested BRCA1 positive (same pathogenic variant, c.5266dupC) - referred for enhanced surveillance (annual breast MRI, pelvic ultrasound, CA-125 until RRSO at appropriate age ~35-40). The younger child (age 19) tested BRCA1 negative. Patient emotional support provided. No dose modifications required at this visit. ECOG performance status: 0. Assessment: Complete response maintained on niraparib maintenance therapy. Plan: Continue niraparib 100 mg daily, CBC q4 weeks, CA-125 and clinical exam q3 months, CT surveillance annually or per symptoms.

At age 49 years (24 months from initial diagnosis, 18 months of niraparib maintenance), annual surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast and CT chest. Findings: No evidence of disease. Expected post-surgical changes from prior debulking surgery. No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity. No pathologically enlarged pelvic or retroperitoneal lymph nodes. No hepatic or splenic lesions. No adnexal masses (bilateral oophorectomy status). CT chest: No pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion, no mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Impression: CONTINUED COMPLETE RESPONSE. No evidence of recurrent high-grade serous carcinoma at 24-month surveillance. Cancer status: NO CANCER IDENTIFIED. Durable complete response on PARP inhibitor maintenance therapy.

At age 49 years (24-month surveillance visit), the patient remains in complete clinical and radiographic remission. CA-125: 12 U/mL (stable within normal limits). HE4: 52 pmol/L (normal). ECOG performance status: 0. CBC: WBC 5.3, Hgb 11.8, Plt 181,000/mcL (excellent hematologic recovery and stability on niraparib 100 mg daily). She has completed 18 months of niraparib maintenance and is on track to complete the planned 3-year (36-month) maintenance course. No cumulative toxicities have emerged. Peripheral neuropathy has fully resolved (Grade 0). The patient asks about the possibility of discontinuing niraparib early; she is counseled that per current evidence (PRIMA/PRIMA-Bin data), completing the full duration maximizes progression-free survival benefit, and she agrees to continue. Menopausal symptoms (surgical menopause): vasomotor symptoms managed with non-hormonal agents (escitalopram 10 mg daily - also has benefit for mood support). Vaginal dryness managed with vaginal moisturizer. Bone density reassessed at 24 months: T-score -1.4 (stable osteopenia, no progression to osteoporosis). Continues calcium and vitamin D. No bisphosphonate indicated at this time. Plan: Continue niraparib 100 mg daily through 36 months from initiation. Surveillance CA-125 and clinical exam every 3 months. Annual CT imaging. Ongoing support for surgical menopause management.

documents.text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
CT CHEST WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
INTERIM RESTAGING EXAMINATION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Marguerite Okonkwo is a 47-year-old female with known FIGO Stage IIIC, pT3cN1M0 high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary (bilateral primary), germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC). She underwent primary debulking surgery with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, total abdominal hysterectomy, radical omentectomy, appendectomy, resection of sigmoid mesenteric implants, right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping, and bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, achieving complete gross resection (R0) with no macroscopic residual disease. She subsequently initiated adjuvant carboplatin AUC 6 / paclitaxel 175 mg/m2 intravenously every 21 days. The current examination is requested as an interim restaging study following completion of cycle 3, with concurrent clinical concern regarding treatment response. Pre-treatment CA-125 was 4,872 U/mL at diagnosis; most recent CA-125 at cycle 2, day 1 was 287 U/mL.

COMPARISON:
Prior CT abdomen, pelvis, and chest obtained at the time of initial diagnosis and staging (prior to primary debulking surgery).

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the uneventful administration of intravenous iohexol contrast. Axial acquisitions were performed through the chest, abdomen, and pelvis in the portal venous phase. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations were generated and reviewed. Oral contrast was administered. Scout images reviewed prior to acquisition.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are clear. No focal consolidation, ground-glass opacity, or airspace disease. No pulmonary nodules or masses identified. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No endobronchial lesions.

Pleural spaces: No pleural effusion bilaterally. No pneumothorax. The pleural surfaces appear smooth without nodularity or thickening.

Mediastinum and hila: The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The mediastinum is midline. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. The great vessels are unremarkable in caliber. The thyroid gland, to the extent imaged, is without focal abnormality.

Chest wall and soft tissues: No suspicious osseous lesion. No soft tissue abnormality in the chest wall. Port-a-cath device is noted in the right chest with the catheter tip appropriately positioned in the distal superior vena cava / cavoatrial junction. No pneumothorax or catheter-related complication.

Right hemidiaphragm: The right hemidiaphragm is intact and smooth in contour. No supradiaphragmatic recurrence or implant identified. Post-procedural change from prior peritoneal stripping of the right hemidiaphragm is noted as subtle thickening and irregularity of the inferior surface of the right hemidiaphragm, unchanged from the post-operative baseline and without evidence of active disease. No right pleural effusion.

---

ABDOMEN:

Ascites: There is complete resolution of the large-volume ascites identified on prior staging imaging, at which time the clinical estimate was approximately 4.5 liters. No free intraperitoneal fluid is identified on the current examination. This represents a marked and complete interval change.

Liver: The liver is normal in size and morphology. The hepatic parenchyma is homogeneous without focal lesion. No hypervascular or hypodense mass. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. The hepatic vasculature is patent. No perihepatic fluid.

Gallbladder and biliary system: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. No cholelithiasis. No biliary ductal dilatation.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size and attenuation without focal lesion. No perisplenic fluid.

Pancreas: The pancreas is normal in morphology and attenuation throughout its length. No pancreatic ductal dilatation. No peripancreatic fat stranding or fluid collection.

Adrenal glands: The bilateral adrenal glands are thin and unremarkable. No adrenal nodule or mass.

Kidneys and ureters: The kidneys are symmetric in size and enhance uniformly without focal parenchymal lesion. No hydronephrosis. No perinephric fluid or stranding. The ureters are not dilated.

Omentum and peritoneum: There is complete resolution of the previously described omental caking. The omentum has been surgically removed and no residual omental tissue is identified. The peritoneal surfaces are smooth and without nodularity. No peritoneal implants are identified along the paracolic gutters, right or left, the undersurface of the right hemidiaphragm, the subhepatic space, the Morrison's pouch, the perisplenic region, or the falciform ligament. Previously described soft tissue nodules along the right and left paracolic gutters are no longer visualized. This represents a complete and favorable interval change from prior.

Para-aortic and retroperitoneal lymph nodes: There is no pathologically enlarged lymph node in the retroperitoneum. The previously noted para-aortic lymph node cluster at the L2 level, which measured up to 1.8 cm in aggregate on the prior staging study, is no longer identifiable as a discrete nodal mass. Residual small, flat lymph nodes in the retroperitoneum do not meet criteria for pathological enlargement. No new retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Surgical changes - abdomen: Post-operative changes consistent with prior midline laparotomy are identified. Post-appendectomy change with no appendix visualized. No bowel obstruction. No ileus. The bowel loops are of normal caliber throughout. No free air. No anastomotic complication.

---

PELVIS:

Adnexal regions: The bilateral adnexa are absent, consistent with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy at the time of primary debulking surgery. No adnexal mass or pelvic soft tissue mass is identified. No recurrence in the adnexal beds.

Uterus: The uterus is absent, consistent with total abdominal hysterectomy. No pelvic soft tissue mass in the expected location of the uterus.

Pelvic peritoneum and cul-de-sac: The pelvic peritoneal surfaces are smooth. No cul-de-sac nodularity. No implants along the rectosigmoid mesentery. The previously described sigmoid mesenteric implants (two implants, largest 1.6 cm) are not visualized, consistent with prior resection and no evidence of local recurrence at the surgical site.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is normally distended with homogeneous urine. No bladder wall thickening. No intraluminal filling defect.

Rectum and sigmoid colon: The rectum and sigmoid colon are unremarkable. No wall thickening. No pericolic fat stranding.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Okonkwo is a 48-year-old woman with FIGO IIIC, T3cN1M0 high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma with germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC), currently on nira maintenance therapy at the reduced dose of 100 mg daily following prior Grade 2 thrombocytopenia requiring a 4-week drug hold. She is now approximately 6 weeks into the reduced dose and doing extremely well by all objective and subjective measures.

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA - COMPLETE REMISSION, ON MAINTENANCE THERAPY

Ms. Okonkwo remains in complete clinical and biochemical remission. CA-125 is 9 U/mL (well within normal), HE4 is 54 pmol/L (normal), and she has no clinical or symptomatic evidence of disease recurrence. Her last CT scan (performed at end of chemotherapy) demonstrated complete radiographic response with no evidence of residual disease; next surveillance CT is scheduled in approximately 6 weeks as part of the 6-month surveillance imaging plan. The combination of R0 primary debulking surgery, complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy, BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant, positive HRD score (62), and CA-125 normalization places her in a highly favorable category for benefit from Zejula maintenance per the PRIMA/ENGOT-OV16 trial data.

Plan:
- Continue nira 100 mg PO daily (individualized reduced maintenance dose - no further dose reduction required given hematologic stability)
- Surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast and CT chest in approximately 6 weeks (scheduled)
- CA-125 and HE4 monitoring every 3 months
- Clinical examination every 3 months
- Reinforce recurrence awareness counseling: patient instructed to contact clinic promptly for abdominal distension, early satiety, pelvic pain, rising CA-125, or any new symptoms

2. Zejula-ASSOCIATED THROMBOCYTOPENIA - RESOLVED

Platelets have fully recovered to 156,000/mcL (Grade 0 per CTCAE v5.0), representing excellent hematologic recovery since the Grade 2 nadir at 98,000/mcL approximately 10 weeks ago. The patient has now been on Zejula 100 mg daily for 6 weeks without recurrence of significant thrombocytopenia. No bleeding symptoms were reported or identified on examination. The dose reduction from 200 mg to 100 mg daily appears to have been the appropriate management strategy, consistent with published data and the FDA label recommendation for Grade 2 thrombocytopenia. Per protocol, if thrombocytopenia were to recur at Grade 2 or above, a further dose reduction to 100 mg (already at this dose) would necessitate discontinuation per label; this was reviewed with the patient.

Plan:
- Continue Zejula 100 mg daily without further modification
- CBC with differential every 4 weeks throughout maintenance therapy
- Platelet counts should be monitored closely, particularly in the setting of any intercurrent illness, new medications, or anticipated changes that could affect bone marrow reserve
- Patient re-educated on signs of bleeding and instructed to call if platelet-related symptoms arise
- No bone marrow biopsy indicated at this time given resolved counts and clinical context

3. CHEMOTHERAPY-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY - IMPROVING, GRADE 1

Residual peripheral neuropathy from PTX is now at Grade 1 (barely perceptible distal toe tingling, no functional impairment). This is a significant improvement from the Grade 2 neuropathy experienced during treatment. Examination is consistent: mildly diminished light touch at the distal toes bilaterally, intact proprioception, symmetric findings. The natural history of pacli-induced neuropathy is gradual improvement over months to years following completion of the offending agent, and her trajectory is appropriately favorable. Zejula does not significantly contribute to peripheral neurotoxicity.

Plan:
- No pharmacologic intervention for neuropathy at Grade 1 (SNRIs, gabapentin, duloxetine deferred unless progression)
- Continue acetyl-L-carnitine per patient preference (evidence limited but low risk)
- Reassess at each visit; if progression to Grade 2, consider duloxetine 30 mg QD titrating to 60 mg
- Neurology referral if worsening or if functional impairment develops

4. MILD ANEMIA - STABLE, IRON DEFICIENCY COMPONENT

Hemoglobin today is 11.4 g/dL, which represents CTCAE Grade 1 anemia (10.0 g/dL to lower limit of normal). This is multifactorial: prior surgical blood loss (estimated 850 mL at debulking), chemotherapy-related erythropoietic suppression, iron deficiency (ferritin of 12 ng/mL, low serum iron, elevated TIBC identified on last CBC workup), and the known myelosuppressive effect of Zejula. Importantly, Hgb is 11.4 g/dL today, which represents a slight improvement from the prior value of 11.0 g/dL when iron supplementation was initiated, consistent with a response to oral iron. MCV is normocytic today at 83 fL, which may reflect mixed-deficiency picture with early iron repletion and concurrent Zejula effect; worth rechecking at next visit.

Plan:
- Continue ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO BID, taken with food to minimize GI intolerance (patient tolerating this approach)
- Recheck CBC with iron studies including ferritin, serum iron, TIBC in 4 weeks
- If Hgb falls below 8.0 g/dL (Grade 3 or severe Grade 2), would consider nira dose hold; currently no need for modification
- Erythropoiesis-stimulating agent not indicated at this time (Grade 1 anemia, asymptomatic, upward trajectory)
- No IV iron ordered at this time given upward hemoglobin trajectory on oral supplementation; will reassess

5. Zejula GI TOXICITY - MANAGED

The patient's nausea is Grade 1 and well-managed with scheduled bedtime ondansetron 8 mg PO 30 minutes prior to Zejula. This is the standard approach and is working effectively. No vomiting. Fatigue is Grade 1, not limiting function, and the patient is working full-time. These are expected and acceptable toxicities at the current reduced dose.

Plan:
- Continue ondansetron 8 mg PO 30 minutes pre-Zejula at bedtime
- PRN ondansetron 4 mg for breakthrough nausea
- Reassess at next visit; if nausea worsens, will consider adding metoclopramide or switching to palonosetron
- Fatigue counseling: light exercise (walking, yoga as tolerated) encouraged, consistent with ASCO guidelines showing benefit in cancer-related fatigue

6. SURGICAL MENOPAUSE MANAGEMENT

The patient underwent bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy as part of her debulking procedure and is in surgically induced menopause. She is managing vasomotor symptoms with non-hormonal approaches (previously discussed). Vaginal dryness is managed with vaginal moisturizer. She continues calcium 1200 mg + vitamin D 1000 IU daily for bone health; baseline DXA scan showed T-score of -1.2 (osteopenia) and she will have a follow-up DXA scan at 24 months from surgery.

Plan:

- Calcium and vitamin D supplementation ongoing
- DXA scan at 24-month post-surgical mark (upcoming)
- If symptomatic worsening of vasomotor symptoms, can reassess SNRIs or consider referral to menopause specialist

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL AND FAMILY GENETIC COUNSELING

Ms. Okonkwo is coping well psychologically. She has returned to full-time work, which she finds meaningful and restorative. Ongoing emotional challenge relates to her older child's BRCA1 positive test result. She is appropriately processing this and is supported by the genetic counseling team and oncology social work. Her younger child tested negative, which has been a source of relief. She understands her own ongoing surveillance and maintenance plan well and is highly engaged in her care.

Plan:

- Patient's own germline testing implications for extended family members discussed; she was encouraged to share results with siblings (she has two siblings) for their consideration of genetic testing

OVERALL IMPRESSION AND SUMMARY:

Ms. Okonkwo is doing remarkably well at this point in her course. She is in complete remission from her FIGO IIIC high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, tolerating Zejula maintenance therapy at the reduced dose of 100 mg daily without significant toxicity, has normalized CA-125 and HE4, is back at full-time work, and demonstrates no clinical or biochemical signs of disease recurrence. Her hematologic parameters are stable and recovering. The treatment course has been challenging but she has managed each complication expertly - from Paraplatin desensitization to nira dose reduction - and she is now on a stable, sustainable maintenance plan.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Ms. Okonkwo is a 47-year-old Nigerian-American woman with no prior personal history of malignancy who presents with a clinical picture that is overwhelmingly consistent with advanced-stage ovarian carcinoma. The combination of bilateral complex adnexal masses, large-volume ascites with omental caking and peritoneal implants on CT, markedly elevated CA-125 at 4,872 U/mL, markedly elevated HE4 at 412 pmol/L, and ROMA high-risk classification, together with her significant family history of ovarian and breast cancer (mother, maternal aunt), is highly concerning for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma. This clinical presentation is classic and the probability of malignancy is extremely high.

Problem List and Plan:

1. BILATERAL COMPLEX ADNEXAL MASSES WITH PERITONEAL CARCINOMATOSIS AND MASSIVE ASCITES - PRESUMED OVARIAN MALIGNANCY:
   - The differential diagnosis at this point is essentially limited to high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma given the clinical gestalt, though primary peritoneal carcinoma and fallopian tube carcinoma (which are managed identically) remain in the differential. A primary GI or appendiceal malignancy (mucinous carcinoma) is less likely given the imaging morphology and the serum tumor marker profile (normal CEA and CA 19-9).
   - Histologic confirmation is required prior to definitive treatment planning. Given the large-volume ascites, diagnostic and therapeutic paracentesis will be arranged on an urgent basis for cytologic evaluation. Cell block will be requested to facilitate immunohistochemical characterization.
   - The case will be discussed at our multidisciplinary gynecologic oncology tumor board.
   - Upfront evaluation of surgical candidacy is ongoing in parallel. Pending tissue confirmation and further laboratory workup, the patient appears to be a reasonable surgical candidate (ECOG 1, good functional status, no significant medical comorbidities).
   - Surgical consultation with our anesthesia colleagues will be arranged for pre-operative risk assessment in anticipation of primary debulking surgery, should this be the recommended approach following tissue diagnosis.

2. MARKEDLY ELEVATED SERUM TUMOR MARKERS (CA-125 4,872 U/mL, HE4 412 pmol/L):
   - These will serve as baseline values for monitoring treatment response.
   - Serial CA-125 measurements will be drawn with each clinical encounter.

3. FAMILY HISTORY STRONGLY SUGGESTIVE OF HEREDITARY BREAST AND OVARIAN CANCER (HBOC) SYNDROME:
   - The patient's family history is remarkable for her mother dying of ovarian cancer (age 55) and a maternal aunt diagnosed with breast cancer at age 42. This pedigree is strongly suggestive of a germline BRCA1 or BRCA2 pathogenic variant.
   - Germline genetic testing will be arranged on an urgent basis (blood draw today for a comprehensive hereditary cancer panel, including BRCA1/2 and additional genes such as RAD51C, RAD51D, BRIP1, PALB2, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, and EPCAM, among others).
   - Genetic counseling has been arranged; the patient will meet with our genetic counselor prior to receiving results. The implications of a BRCA1/2 pathogenic variant for her two children (aged 22 and 19) were introduced today at a high level; the patient was highly receptive. Full counseling will be deferred to the formal genetic counseling session.
   - Regardless of tumor histology, somatic tumor molecular profiling will be pursued (NGS-based testing on tumor tissue) for prognostic and therapeutic planning purposes, including BRCA somatic status, homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) scoring, and tumor mutational burden.

   - The patient has massive ascites estimated at approximately 4.5 liters clinically. She reports moderate abdominal discomfort and early satiety attributable to the ascites.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HIGH-GRADE SEROUS OVARIAN CARCINOMA, FIGO STAGE IIIC, BRCA1 GERMLINE PATHOGENIC VARIANT - COMPLETE REMISSION:
   Ms. Okonkwo is doing extremely well at 24 months from diagnosis. She is in durable complete remission across all parameters: imaging demonstrates no evidence of disease, CA-125 is stable at 12 U/mL (well within normal limits), and she is clinically asymptomatic. This represents a gratifying and expected outcome in a BRCA1-mutated patient who achieved R0 resection followed by platinum-based chemotherapy and PARP inhibitor maintenance. She has now completed 18 of the planned 36 months of niraparib maintenance. The magnitude of benefit from PARP inhibitor maintenance in patients with germline BRCA1/2-mutated ovarian cancer achieving complete response to first-line platinum is substantial, with hazard ratios for progression-free survival in the range of 0.3-0.4 in trials such as SOLO-1 and PRIMA. Completing the full planned duration remains strongly supported by available evidence.
   
   Regarding the patient's question about early discontinuation: I reviewed with Ms. Okonkwo the current data landscape. In the SOLO-1 trial, the benefit of olaparib maintenance in BRCA-mutated patients who achieved complete response was sustained out to 7-year follow-up with a significant improvement in overall survival. The PRIMA data for niraparib likewise support completing the planned maintenance duration. There is no evidence that early discontinuation in a responding patient is safe from an oncologic standpoint, and there is theoretical concern that stopping early could allow microscopic residual disease - if present - to resume growth. I acknowledge her excellent response and the burden of long-term therapy, and I emphasized that at 100 mg daily she has demonstrated good tolerability with stable hematologic parameters for many months. She heard this clearly, expressed understanding, and agreed to continue niraparib for the planned 36-month course. I will continue to reassess at each visit.
   Plan: Continue niraparib (Zejula) 100 mg PO once daily at bedtime. No dose modification indicated at this time given stable CBC and continued tolerability. CBC monitoring every 4 weeks. CA-125 and HE4 every 3 months. Next annual CT surveillance scan in 12 months (at 36-month disease mark), or sooner if symptoms develop.

2. NIRAPARIB MAINTENANCE TOLERABILITY:
   At 100 mg daily, the patient is tolerating niraparib well. Platelets remain stable at 181,000/mcL - well above the prior nadir - without any recurrence of thrombocytopenia at the reduced dose, which is a favorable and expected pattern. Hemoglobin of 11.8 g/dL reflects continued slow improvement (was 11.5 at last visit), consistent with both iron repletion effect and intrinsic hematopoietic recovery. Nausea has resolved; she no longer requires scheduled ondansetron premedication, though she has been counseled to use it PRN if nausea recurs. Fatigue is at Grade 0-1, not impairing her daily function.
   Plan: Continue as above. Repeat CBC at 4-week interval. If any hematologic changes arise (particularly platelet decline), will reassess and manage per protocol. No further dose reduction planned at this time.

3. IRON DEFICIENCY ANEMIA - RESOLVING:
   Iron studies confirm improved ferritin (28 ng/mL, up from 12 ng/mL) and transferrin saturation trending upward toward normal. Hemoglobin is mildly improved. Continue oral ferrous sulfate; will transition to maintenance dose (ferrous sulfate 325 mg every other day or reduced to once daily, as she has already reduced from BID per my prior note) once ferritin exceeds 30-40 ng/mL. Recheck iron studies in 3 months.
   Plan: Continue ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO once daily. Recheck iron panel at next visit in 3 months.

4. SURGICAL MENOPAUSE / VASOMOTOR SYMPTOMS:
   Hot flashes have partially improved on escitalopram 10 mg daily. She still has 3-4 hot flashes per day and 2-3 episodes of night sweats per week, which she describes as manageable and improved from the prior visit. Given her BRCA1 status and ovarian cancer history, hormone replacement therapy is generally avoided (though some guidelines note that short-term low-dose HRT is not absolutely contraindicated following R0 resection for ovarian cancer, particularly in young women, given the LIBERATE and GOG data - however, this patient has not requested HRT and the benefit-risk discussion has not identified a compelling indication to initiate it at this time). Non-hormonal management will continue. If vasomotor symptoms worsen or substantially affect sleep quality, will consider dose escalation of escitalopram or addition of an alternative non-hormonal agent such as venlafaxine or gabapentin.
   Plan: Continue escitalopram 10 mg daily. Continue Replens vaginal moisturizer. Revisit in 3 months.

5. OSTEOPENIA:
   DXA at 18 months showed T-score -1.4 (stable from -1.2 at 12 months). She is on calcium 1200 mg daily and vitamin D 1000 IU daily. BMI 23.5 and weight-bearing activity (she walks as part of her daily routine) provide additional protection. At present, T-score is in the osteopenia range but not yet osteoporosis (<-2.5). Bisphosphonate therapy is not indicated at this time but will be reassessed at next DXA. Next DXA should be performed at the 36-month mark (one year from today).
   Plan: Continue calcium carbonate 1200 mg PO daily and vitamin D3 1000 IU PO daily. Encourage continued weight-bearing activity. Repeat DXA in 12 months.

6. HYPOTHYROIDISM / HYPERTENSION:
   Both comorbidities remain well-controlled per patient report and TSH of 1.8 mIU/L. Blood pressure 128/76 on amlodipine 5 mg - excellent control. Of note, she is not on bevacizumab (was deferred at time of treatment planning given pre-existing hypertension and patient preference), so hypertension management is straightforward. No medication changes needed.
   Plan: Continue levothyroxine and amlodipine at current doses.

The patient remains understandably concerned for her older child. I acknowledged this and provided reassurance that the genetic team has the protocols in place: annual breast MRI starting now (given age and BRCA1 status), discussion of chemoprevention, and consideration of RRSO at approximately 35-40 years of age when childbearing is likely complete. The patient is grateful for the proactive management. No action required from gynecologic oncology directly.

IMPRESSION

1. LARGE BILATERAL COMPLEX ADNEXAL MASSES: A large right adnexal mass (9.2 x 7.4 x 8.1 cm) and a left adnexal mass (4.8 x 3.9 x 4.1 cm), both demonstrating mixed solid-cystic morphology with thick irregular septations, internal papillary projections, and enhancing solid components. This constellation of features is highly suspicious for bilateral primary ovarian or fallopian tube malignancy, most likely high-grade serous carcinoma given the clinical and laboratory context.

2. MASSIVE ASCITES: Very large volume free intraperitoneal fluid distributed throughout the abdomen and pelvis, estimated 4-5 liters. Malignant etiology strongly suspected in this clinical setting.

3. OMENTAL CAKING: Diffuse replacement of the greater omentum by lobular confluent soft-tissue infiltration (omental cake), most prominent in the right upper abdomen with omental thickness up to 3.2 cm. This pattern is characteristic of peritoneal carcinomatosis from an ovarian primary.

4. PERITONEAL CARCINOMATOSIS: Multiple soft-tissue peritoneal implants distributed along the right and left paracolic gutters, the pelvic peritoneum including the rectouterine pouch, the sigmoid mesentery (two discrete implants up to 1.3 cm), the right hemidiaphragm (0.7 cm subdiaphragmatic nodule), and along the anterior hepatic capsular surface (0.6 cm). The distribution is characteristic of advanced peritoneal dissemination from a gynecologic primary.

5. REGIONAL LYMPHADENOPATHY: Enlarged right external iliac lymph node (2.1 cm) and a right para-aortic/interaortocaval nodal cluster (aggregate 1.8 cm) at the L2 level, both morphologically suspicious for nodal metastatic involvement. Borderline left external iliac and left para-aortic nodes are indeterminate.

6. NO DISTANT (M1) DISEASE IDENTIFIED: No hepatic parenchymal metastases. No pulmonary nodules or pulmonary metastases. No pleural effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No osseous metastases in the visualized skeleton.

Overall, the CT findings are highly characteristic of advanced-stage bilateral ovarian carcinoma with diffuse peritoneal spread, omental cake, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node involvement, and massive malignant ascites. The absence of hepatic parenchymal metastases, pulmonary involvement, or distant disease beyond the peritoneum is consistent with FIGO Stage IIIC disease. Clinical correlation and tissue sampling are advised to establish histologic confirmation.

GROSS DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Received in the laboratory is a large-volume peritoneal fluid specimen labeled with patient identifiers, submitted in two standard cytology collection containers. The total volume is 2.8 liters. The fluid is straw-colored to slightly turbid in appearance with no appreciable blood-tinged component. No gross tissue fragments, fibrinous clots, or particulate debris are identified macroscopically. The specimen was processed as follows: centrifugation at 1500 rpm for 10 minutes yielded a moderate cellular pellet. A cytospin preparation was made from 10 mL of the unspun supernatant. The remaining fluid was submitted for concentration and cell block preparation using the Cellient automated cell block method. Two Diff-Quik-stained and two Papanicolaou-stained cytospin slides were prepared. A formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) cell block was prepared from the centrifuged pellet and submitted for routine hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining and immunohistochemical analysis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Cytospin Preparations (Papanicolaou Stain and Diff-Quik):
The cytospin preparations are highly cellular. The background contains a moderate amount of proteinaceous debris, scattered reactive mesothelial cells, lymphocytes, and macrophages. Admixed throughout the preparation are numerous atypical epithelial cells, present as both single cells and, most strikingly, as three-dimensional papillary clusters and tight morules (cellular balls). These atypical clusters demonstrate a characteristic architectural pattern with cells at the periphery showing peripheral palisading and a central fibrovascular-like core in some aggregates.

The individual malignant cells are large and polygonal with abundant eosinophilic to amphophilic cytoplasm. Nuclear features are markedly abnormal: nuclei are significantly enlarged and pleomorphic, with irregular, angulated nuclear membranes. Nucleoli are large, prominent, and eccentrically placed, sometimes appearing macronucleolar. The nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio is markedly increased. Multinucleation and bizarrely shaped nuclei are observed in a minority of cells. Mitotic figures are identified, including atypical mitoses. No psammomatous calcifications are identified on the cytospin preparation; however, rare calcific fragments are seen on the cell block sections (see below).

Cell Block (H&E Sections):
Multiple histologic sections through the cell block demonstrate sheets and papillary clusters of pleomorphic malignant epithelial cells embedded within a fibrinous, eosinophilic background matrix. The architectural arrangement includes solid nests, papillary formations with fibrovascular cores, and occasional cribriform areas. Individual cells display the cytomorphologic features described above. Rare psammoma body-like calcifications are present within the papillary clusters. The mitotic rate is brisk (estimated >10 mitoses per 10 high-power fields in representative areas). No glandular lumina with intraluminal mucin are identified. No squamous differentiation is present. The morphologic features are those of a high-grade carcinoma with serous-type differentiation, consistent with high-grade serous carcinoma.

  CK7 (clone OV-TL 12/30):           POSITIVE - diffuse cytoplasmic staining, strong intensity

  Estrogen Receptor / ER (clone SP1): POSITIVE (focal) - positive nuclear staining in approximately 15-20% of tumor cells, weak to moderate intensity
  p53 (clone DO-7):                  ABERRANT PATTERN - diffuse strong nuclear positivity ("block-type") in >80% of tumor cells; this pattern is consistent with missense gain-of-function TP53 mutation
  p16 (clone E6H4):                  POSITIVE - diffuse block positivity ("block-type" pattern) in the vast majority of tumor cells; consistent with p16 overexpression as seen in high-grade serous carcinoma
  Ki-67 (clone MIB-1):               HIGH - proliferative index approximately 70% of tumor nuclei staining positive
  BRCA1 (clone MS110):               LOSS OF NUCLEAR EXPRESSION in tumor cells; intact nuclear staining in admixed stromal cells and reactive mesothelial cells (internal positive control intact); loss of BRCA1 protein expression in tumor cells noted

DIAGNOSIS

PERITONEAL FLUID (ASCITIC FLUID), ULTRASOUND-GUIDED THERAPEUTIC AND DIAGNOSTIC PARACENTESIS:

  POSITIVE FOR MALIGNANT CELLS.

  Morphology and immunophenotype (PAX8+, WT1+, CK7+, CK20-, ER focally+, p53 aberrant/diffuse block-positive pattern, p16 diffuse block-positive, Ki-67 ~70%) are consistent with HIGH-GRADE SEROUS CARCINOMA, most likely of ovarian or fallopian tube primary.

  BRCA1 immunohistochemistry demonstrates loss of nuclear protein expression in tumor cells with intact expression in the internal stromal and mesothelial cell controls.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
COMMENT
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The cytomorphologic features and immunohistochemical profile of the malignant cells in this peritoneal fluid specimen are classic for high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC). The combination of PAX8 and WT1 positivity strongly supports Mullerian (ovarian/fallopian tube/primary peritoneal) origin, effectively excluding non-gynecologic primaries such as metastatic gastrointestinal adenocarcinoma (CK20-negative, WT1-positive pattern) or mesothelioma (WT1 positive, but PAX8 negative in mesothelioma; clinical and morphologic features also inconsistent). The p53 diffuse block-positive staining pattern is characteristic of TP53 missense mutation, which is present in essentially all high-grade serous carcinomas and further supports this diagnosis. The diffuse block-type p16 positivity is also consistent with HGSC.

The elevated Ki-67 proliferative index (~70%) is consistent with high-grade histology.

Loss of BRCA1 nuclear protein expression by IHC in the context of a strongly positive internal mesothelial/stromal cell control is a finding that warrants correlation with germline genetic testing (results pending per separate order). If confirmed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant is identified, this would have significant therapeutic implications including eligibility for PARP inhibitor therapy.

Clinical correlation with cross-sectional imaging findings and serum tumor markers (CA-125: 4,872 U/mL; HE4: 412 pmol/L) is recommended. Final histologic classification and FIGO staging will require correlation with surgical pathology from planned debulking surgery.

FINDING 1 (PATHOGENIC - ACTIONABLE):

Classification: CLASS 5 - PATHOGENIC
Variant Type: Frameshift insertion (single cytosine duplication)
Historical Nomenclature: BRCA1 5382insC (legacy designation); also referenced in literature as "5382insC founder mutation"
Chromosomal Location: 17q21.31
Variant Allele Frequency (germline blood): 48.2% (consistent with germline heterozygosity; expected ~50% in diploid tissue)
zygosity: Heterozygous

Interpretation:
BRCA1 c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74) is a well-characterized pathogenic frameshift variant resulting from the insertion of a single cytosine at nucleotide position 5266 within exon 20 of BRCA1. This insertion causes a translational frameshift leading to a premature termination codon 74 codons downstream (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74), predicting the production of a severely truncated, non-functional BRCA1 protein that is subject to nonsense-mediated mRNA decay. Loss of BRCA1 function eliminates its critical role in high-fidelity homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair, rendering cells reliant on error-prone repair pathways and conferring genomic instability.

This variant is also known historically as "5382insC" (numbered according to legacy cDNA reference sequences) and represents one of the most extensively studied BRCA1 founder mutations in the world. It is highly prevalent in individuals of Ashkenazi Jewish descent (carrier frequency approximately 1 in 40), individuals of Polish and Eastern European ancestry, and has also been identified in West African and other populations at lower frequencies. The variant has been reported in thousands of individuals and families with BRCA1-associated hereditary breast and ovarian cancer (HBOC) syndrome and is definitively classified as pathogenic per ACMG/AMP variant interpretation guidelines (supporting criteria: PVS1, PS1, PP5 - multiple lines of evidence).

Lifetime Cancer Risk Estimates Associated with BRCA1 Pathogenic Variant (heterozygous carrier):
  - Female breast cancer (lifetime to age 80): 72% (range 65-79%)
  - Ovarian cancer (lifetime to age 80): 44% (range 36-53%)
  - Contralateral breast cancer (after ipsilateral diagnosis): ~40% at 10 years
  - Pancreatic cancer: modestly elevated (~2-3x general population)
  - Prostate cancer (in male carriers): elevated risk (for family members)

Clinical Implications:
This pathogenic BRCA1 variant is the causative germline mutation underlying this patient's high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma. Patients with germline BRCA1/2 mutations and ovarian cancer are candidates for PARP inhibitor therapy, including both first-line maintenance (niraparib, olaparib) and treatment (rucaparib, olaparib) settings. Platinum sensitivity is also expected to be enhanced in BRCA1/2-mutated tumors. Cascade genetic testing is strongly recommended for first- and second-degree biological relatives.

METHODOLOGY - SOMATIC (Foundation One CDx)
================================================================================
DNA was extracted from FFPE omental biopsy specimen. Library preparation and hybrid-capture based next-generation sequencing was performed targeting the full coding exons of 324 cancer-relevant genes, select introns of 36 genes frequently rearranged in cancer, and full coding exons of select RNA targets. Mean sequencing depth: >500x (median coverage >800x across all exons). Variant calling: SNVs (VAF >= 5%), indels (VAF >= 5%), copy number amplifications/deletions (>= 6 copies for amplification, <= 1 copy for deletion), gene rearrangements/fusions. Tumor mutational burden calculated from 1.1 Mb of sequenced genome. MSI status determined from 114 microsatellite loci. HRD analysis performed using genome-wide LOH, TAI, and LST scoring (validated algorithm). Results subject to germline filtering using matched normal methodology and population allele frequency databases (gnomAD, 1000 Genomes). Analytical sensitivity for somatic SNVs: >99% at VAF >= 5% with adequate tumor purity. FDA PMA approved as companion diagnostic for multiple PARP inhibitor and other targeted therapy indications (P170019).

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Okonkwo is a 47-year-old Nigerian-American female, G2P2, non-smoker, with a significant family history of ovarian and breast cancer, who presents for initial gynecologic oncology consultation following a new diagnosis of bilateral ovarian masses with peritoneal carcinomatosis. This is her first visit to our department.

The patient reports a 3-month history of progressive abdominal distension, early satiety, and new pelvic pressure. She additionally describes a 9-pound unintentional weight loss over the past 2 months and intermittent right-sided pelvic pain with radiation to the right lower back. These symptoms prompted a visit to her gynecologist approximately 2 weeks ago. At that visit, bimanual examination was notable for a palpable right adnexal mass, and pelvic ultrasound revealed complex bilateral adnexal masses with internal septations, papillary projections, and large-volume free fluid. She was urgently referred to our clinic.

External workup was obtained prior to this visit. CT of the abdomen/pelvis and chest with IV contrast demonstrated a large right adnexal complex mass measuring 9.2 x 7.4 cm and a left adnexal mass measuring 4.8 x 3.9 cm, massive ascites (approximately 4.5 liters), diffuse omental caking, peritoneal implants along the paracolic gutters, pelvic peritoneum, sigmoid mesentery, and right hemidiaphragm, as well as bilateral pelvic and para-aortic lymphadenopathy. No liver parenchymal lesions. No pleural effusion. Dedicated CT chest was without pulmonary metastases or mediastinal adenopathy. Serum tumor markers were markedly abnormal: CA-125 4,872 U/mL, HE4 412 pmol/L, ROMA score consistent with high risk. CEA 2.1 ng/mL (within normal limits).

Diagnostic and therapeutic paracentesis was performed, yielding 2.8 liters of ascitic fluid. Cytology returned POSITIVE FOR MALIGNANT CELLS with morphology and immunophenotype (PAX8+, WT1+, CK7+, CK20-, p53 aberrant/diffuse strong, p16 block positive, ER focally positive, Ki-67 ~70%) consistent with high-grade serous carcinoma of ovarian/fallopian tube primary. BRCA1 IHC demonstrated loss of nuclear expression in tumor cells.

Germline genetic testing was expedited given the family history. Results from the Myriad myRisk 35-gene panel returned a PATHOGENIC VARIANT in BRCA1: c.5266dupC (p.Gln1756ProfsTer74), a Class 5 pathogenic frameshift variant (5382insC founder mutation). A BRCA2 variant of uncertain significance was also identified (c.8167G>A, p.Asp2723Asn), which is not actionable at this time. Somatic tumor NGS (Foundation One CDx) on the omental biopsy cell block was sent and results are available: TP53 p.R175H (68% VAF), BRCA1 somatic LOH (biallelic inactivation with germline), RB1 homozygous deletion, CCNE1 amplification (CN 12), PIK3CA p.E545K (14% VAF, subclonal), NF1 frameshift (22% VAF), CDK12 amplification. HRD score 62 (positive, above threshold of 42). TMB 6.2 mut/Mb. MSS. PD-L1 CPS 5%. No KRAS/NRAS/BRAF mutations. The tumor is classified as HRD-positive and BRCA1-deficient, with actionable PARP inhibitor sensitivity.

The clinical presentation, imaging, cytology, and molecular data are consistent with FIGO Stage IIIC high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary (bilateral), T3cN1M0. This case was formally reviewed at this week's gynecologic oncology multidisciplinary tumor board (MTB), which included representation from gynecologic oncology, medical oncology, diagnostic radiology, pathology, and radiation oncology.

The patient is accompanied today by her husband. She is emotionally distressed but engaged, asking clear and thoughtful questions. She is a high school principal and describes herself as someone who needs to fully understand her situation to process it.

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD SUMMARY:

This case was formally presented at the gynecologic oncology multidisciplinary tumor board this week. Attendees included gynecologic oncology, medical oncology, diagnostic radiology (imaging review), gynecologic pathology, and social work. The following points were discussed and consensus reached:

1. Diagnosis confirmed: FIGO Stage IIIC, T3cN1M0 high-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary (bilateral), consistent with cytologic, immunophenotypic, and molecular findings. Fallopian tube primary suspected given STIC lesions likely at fimbriae (to be confirmed at time of surgery).

2. Molecular profile: Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC) with somatic BRCA1 LOH confirmed. HRD positive (score 62). These findings confirm platinum and PARP inhibitor sensitivity and have direct implications for maintenance therapy planning.

3. Surgical feasibility: The board's radiologic assessment concluded that despite the extensive peritoneal burden and right hemidiaphragm involvement, the disease distribution does not include unresectable lesions (no liver parenchymal disease, no bowel obstruction, no suprarenal lymphadenopathy requiring extensive vascular dissection, no pulmonary involvement). A Fagotti score was estimated from imaging at approximately 6-8, at the margin - however, the gynecologic oncologist is experienced with complex cytoreductive procedures and the board consensus supported attempting primary debulking surgery (PDS) with curative intent and goal of R0 resection, rather than neoadjuvant chemotherapy.

4. Planned adjuvant therapy: Following R0 resection, the plan is for 6 cycles of Paraplatin/Taxol (standard of care), followed by PARP inhibitor maintenance. Given her BRCA1 mutation and anticipated complete response, Zejula is the preferred maintenance agent per current NCCN guidelines and PRIMA/ENGOT-OV16 data. Avastin will be discussed with the patient but given her pre-existing hypertension, may be deferred.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Assessment:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 47-year-old woman with a new diagnosis of FIGO Stage IIIC, AJCC T3cN1M0 high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (bilateral), with peritoneal carcinomatosis, omental caking, massive ascites, and bilateral pelvic/para-aortic lymphadenopathy. Cytology confirms malignant serous cells. Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC) is confirmed. Tumor is HRD-positive by somatic NGS. ECOG performance status 1. No distant (M1) disease. She is a good surgical candidate.

Plan:

1. PRIMARY DEBULKING SURGERY (PDS): The patient has been counseled regarding the goals and risks of primary debulking surgery. We discussed at length the rationale for PDS vs. neoadjuvant chemotherapy (NACT) approach. Given her ECOG 1 status, absence of liver parenchymal metastases, absence of unresectable extra-abdominal disease, and favorable imaging for surgical resectability, we are recommending upfront PDS with a goal of complete gross resection (R0). The evidence base supporting R0 resection as the most important prognostic factor in Stage III/IV ovarian cancer was reviewed with the patient. Planned procedure: total abdominal hysterectomy, bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy, radical omentectomy, pelvic and para-aortic lymph node dissection, appendectomy, resection of all visible peritoneal implants including sigmoid mesenteric implants and right hemidiaphragm peritoneal stripping as needed. Bowel resection is possible but will be determined intraoperatively - colorectal surgery is on standby. The patient was counseled regarding anticipated operative time (5-7 hours), estimated blood loss, need for possible intraoperative transfusion, and standard post-operative course (approximately 5-7 days hospitalization). Risks discussed including bleeding, infection, wound dehiscence, DVT/PE (enoxaparin prophylaxis post-op), bladder/ureteral injury, bowel injury/leak, ileus, and the possibility that R0 resection cannot be achieved intraoperatively, in which case NACT will be initiated.

   - Pre-operative CA-125 level confirmed: 4,872 U/mL (will serve as baseline for response monitoring)
   - Anesthesia pre-operative evaluation: completed and surgical clearance obtained
   - Type and screen obtained; 4 units pRBC on hold
   - Bowel prep to be completed pre-operatively
   - Surgical consent obtained today; patient verbalized understanding of risks and benefits
   - Surgery scheduled for within the next 7-10 days (OR block confirmed)

2. GENETIC COUNSELING:
   - Germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC) confirmed. This has direct therapeutic implications (PARP inhibitor eligibility post-treatment) and significant familial implications.
   - The patient has two children, ages 22 and 19, each at 50% risk of having inherited this variant. Genetic counseling referral placed for the patient and for her children.
   - Prophylactic risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy (RRSO) for affected family members was discussed as a future consideration at appropriate ages (~35-40 years).
   - Patient appropriately distressed but engaged regarding familial implications. She has already discussed this diagnosis with her husband and is prepared to discuss with her children.

3. ADJUVANT CHEMOTHERAPY PLANNING (post-surgical):
   - Following R0 resection and adequate post-operative recovery (target: 4-6 weeks post-op), the plan is to initiate platinum-based doublet chemotherapy: carbo AUC 6 / pacli 175 mg/m2 IV q21 days x 6 cycles.
   - Paraplatin dosing will be calculated using the Calvert formula (GFR by CKD-EPI or 24-hr urine creatinine clearance; current eGFR ~68 mL/min).
   - BSA 1.74 m2 - Taxol dose will be approximately 304 mg; carbo dose approximately 612 mg (will be recalculated at time of treatment).
   - Port-a-cath placement will be discussed and planned prior to initiation of chemotherapy.
   - Regarding bev: given her pre-existing well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine, the risk of bev-associated worsening hypertension, proteinuria, and other vascular events was discussed. After shared decision-making, the patient expressed preference to proceed without bev at this time, consistent with the available data showing the primary benefit of Avastin is in PFS rather than OS in this population. Final decision to be confirmed after surgery once pathologic data are fully available.

4. MAINTENANCE THERAPY PLANNING:
   - Given confirmed germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant, HRD-positive tumor, and anticipated complete response to platinum-based chemotherapy, the plan (barring unexpected circumstances) is to initiate nira (Zejula) maintenance monotherapy following completion of adjuvant chemotherapy.
   - Per PRIMA/ENGOT-OV16 trial data and current FDA approval, Zejula is indicated as first-line maintenance therapy in patients with BRCA-mutated or HRD-positive high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma in complete or partial response to first-line platinum-based chemotherapy.
   - Patient educated regarding individualized starting dose strategy based on weight (<77 kg); will be finalized at time of maintenance initiation.
   - Baseline CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel, and creatinine will be confirmed prior to initiating Zejula.

5. PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT:
   - Referral placed to oncology social work for emotional support and practical assistance (sick leave planning, family communication support, financial counseling).
   - Patient and husband counseled extensively today. She is anxious but demonstrates good coping and realistic expectations. She understands the severity of the diagnosis but is encouraged by the planned treatment approach.
   - She specifically asked about return to work as a school principal - counseled that chemotherapy will require activity modification during treatment, but long-term functional recovery is a realistic goal.
   - Nutrition consult placed given mild hypoalbuminemia (3.2 g/dL) and pre-surgical nutritional optimization.

6. FOLLOW-UP:
   - Patient to return for pre-operative visit in 5 days for final pre-op clearance.
   - Post-operatively, follow-up in clinic at 4 weeks to assess recovery and initiate adjuvant chemotherapy planning.
   - CA-125 and HE4 will be rechecked at 4-week post-op visit as pre-chemotherapy baseline.

Time spent with patient and family: approximately 75 minutes, including comprehensive counseling regarding diagnosis, treatment plan, genetic implications, and questions from patient and husband. Multidisciplinary case discussion reviewed. All questions answered to the best of our ability.
